commentBody:
I haven't seen a Goto statement in code in a long time, thankfully.   

Some great clueing in this one!

I cackled aloud at the Jeopardy clue. And then had to apologize to my cat.  

I sent myself astray thinking the instrument would be either lute or lyre, so I helpfully filled in the first and last letter. That got me stuck in that section until I deleted the l and the e...
Ken Jennings should have been given credit for that answer.  It wasn't wrong!
Excellent across and down clues. We laughed out loud at the “or” puns. (This is my first comment ever.)
Fun,  but kind of easy. Though I was temporarily misled on the theme when I saw TENORT_____ and filled in TENNORTenNer, which could have also been a fun theme.

I got a nice kick out of that!  A very nice kick, indeed!   And not one in the European bum.

I wasn't super-fast at figuring out what was going on with the theme answers, but the puzzle gave me a nice shot of those cool brain chemicals you get when something falls into place.

I am a HUGE fan of [Blue stop sign?]  Just excellent wordplay.

11D was funny in a bizarre way, which is often my favorite way.

Very nice done, Mr. Kugelman.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Looking forward to the irate responses which will be generated by the misogynistic secondary cluing of the long-handled garden tool.

Another wonderless and straightforward Kugelman confection for me.

Wonderful, John Kugelman!

Just goes to show you - a little knowledge CAN BE helpful...

It was bound to happen - a grid would come around with all the right odds and sods to scratch an (almost) esoteric itch!

Pure TEXASGOLD!

This was a Thursday puzzle???

Felt like a Wednesday - maybe the NYT is also lagging this week after the 3-day weekend…

I'm planning on spending a night in Dodge, Kansas this fall. It amused me to no end to learn that Dodge is the Seat of Ford County. The military officers who lent their names to the town and county are unrelated to the respective car makers

A disappointment.  Everything so easy, and subpar answers such as "main" and "oh no reason".  As to the latter, what the devil?  It's just a terrible clue and answer set, unless I'm missing something clever.  "Written with venom" sure seems like the wrong tense, and while overarm seems awkward and alien to me (as it did the columnist) perhaps it's more common in sportsball circles.  The theme was paper thin.  Latest in a long recent trend of uninteresting Thursdays.  :(

So first, let me be clear, I thoroughly enjoyed this puzzle on a lot of levels. A great theme which was laugh out loud and clear as crystal.  Wonderful, interesting accessory fill like SLOEEYED, OHNOREASON, MENAGERIE and SAFEWORD  (used fairly recently I think, but probably well after John submitted this, and with a fun fresh clue). Also a lot of clever clues elsewhere.
Still I’m curious if people will agree with me that this is more a Wednesday theme instead of Thursday. And I felt for me it solved like a Wednesday too. This could be one of those days where I really was on the constructor’s wavelength—I got both OWL and WING 
right away despite the misdirection, and just spent the day in Clam Pass Beach in Naples where I watched a clam SIDLE past me, so got that without crosses; and anyone my age who watched “The Beverly Hillbillies” would probably fill in TEXASTEA as soon as the X in NIX fell. Still this was a 12 minute solve for me. I don’t usually mention my times and this is not a brag; I am not a speed solver and this is an unusually fast time for me for a Thursday or even a Wednesday, so wondering if others had as many gimmes as I did, or if it’s just me today.
Still, bottom line, I always enjoy a John Kugelman puzzle (despite his being your sworn nemesis, Andrzej) and today was no exception, so forgive my musings about the challenge because that shouldn’t take away from the overall achievement of a great puzzle, theme and grid.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Not for nothing, but isn't the pejorative/celebratory/tongue-in-cheek (that one's for Andrzej) homophone of "hoe" actually "ho?"

So many problems with 16A.

Any puzzle with OMAR and NINA in it is okay by me. 
Because it's a Thursday puzzle i was wary most of the time solving, but enjoyed it despite no tricks. 
Thanks John.
@Sean and various others. In this part of the world it has always been OVERARM, with related sidearm, (I believe seen in baseball pitching) and underarm (no longer seen in bowling in cricket)
Outfielders where flies are hit wear gloves not mitts.
Meh puzzle for a Thursday. Pretty much ruined by 40A.

As always, I was not on this constructor's wavelength (I find his clues to be joyless stretches rather than clever misdirection), I didn't know the trivia (of which, as is usual with him, there was a slew), and I thought the theme was meh (at least the dad joke factor was slightly below Kugelman's average - the single, minor blessing today). Still, I completed the puzzle in Tuesday/Wednesday time 🤷🏽

Why does my least favorite constructor appear to have the most puzzles published by the NYT... And on Thursday, too, which is the day I wait for to be intelligently challenged by an inventive theme and smart clues 😢

a clever theme that didn’t become clear to me until i’d gotten all the clues lol. this one felt awkward—POISON PEN and OH NO REASON in particular seemed a bit clunky. (i agree with other commenters that i don’t quite see how “written with…” and a noun match as parts of speech). 

new words learned, however—lothario! great off the tounge.
I enjoyed figuring out the theme and theme answers which surely accelerated my solve time which was 40% less than average.
Just gonna say that I happened to be doing a puzzle in the 2024 archives the other day, and was so pleasantly surprised that a clue used the term "sex worker."
Nice puzzle, Mr. Kugelman.  Not a killer, but cleverly clued. The theme fills made me smile every time, and I'm trying to lighten up these days. Thank you!

That was a delight. Loved the theme, and fantastic cluing. (32A)Blue stop sign solving to Safe word? Genius! (30A) type of drag solving to main, great aha moment. (31A)Parrot solving to ape? Simple and elegant. Loved this one

Unlike almost everyone commenting before, I enjoyed this puzzle and thought the theme phrases were clever and fun. Easy compared to  my Thursday expectations, but it made me laugh a couple times and that made up for it. I didn't dislike the 'oh, no reason' entry as others apparently did. I don't know what was so objectionable - it's something I might have said when I was a kid (I wasn't a quick thinker.:-)

Amusing, but easy Thursday puzzle, finished in Tuesday-Wednesday time. Liked the theme answers, got temporarily fooled by some of the misdirection, but it eventually got worked out before finishing.

Thanks, John.

I rather liked it! Very fun theme and some enjoyable entries. Hello, HAR, my old friend....  😏

I feel so seen!  Har! 

And I rather liked the coy little OHNOREASON. Lalalaaa. Nothing to see here... Need help with dinner, mom?

That said, I was real SLOWEYED, err, I mean slow. Looking back, I've no idea why.  

Suppose it didn't help that though I filled them in right away, I managed to mispell both GANDHI and OHIOAN. Not exactly strange words to me, so that was strange.

All good, forward HOE!

Fun play on words. Don’t understand why it’s a Thursday though. Not because of difficulty. But play on words seems very Mon-Wed?

It was clever, fun and quick. 
I don’t quite get “bit of a lark”. WING. like literally wing as part of a bird?  

I don’t know menagerie or lothario or sensate means, though I’ve heard the words before. 


Again. Fun clueing. Nice therme.
"Senseis"?? A sensei can be a teacher of anything, and is never pluralized with an "s".
What was this? Because, as enjoyable as it was to do, it wasn’t a Thursday puzzle.

I got a kick out of the theme, especially TENOR TWENTY.  Very clever. 

Thanks, John. A good start to the day. 

[Lady of the estate]

MANOR WOMAN

How did the mansplainer die? 

He died in a WELL, actually...

I’d like to credit Stephen Colbert for my correctly guessing WACO — i knew it because on his last* show he mentioned that Waco has the largest Dr Pepper bottle in the world, and it’s what they really really want to be known for…

*I really wanted to write “latest”

PS ARSE still makes me wince — for a Brit it really seems much too rude for the Gray Lady!

Loved it! 
I don’t care if it’s a Wednesday or Thursday ease puzzle. It was a great theme, and some great tricky questions. 

Can someone please dumb down for me “oh no reason”? ?  Thanks.

39D [Comes on little cat feet]

Then there's Carl Sandburg's famous poem. About Chicago. He called it a haiku.

Fog
By Carl Sandburg

The fog comes
on little cat feet.

It sits looking
over harbor and city
on silent haunches
and then moves on.

[Outfit for the highly motivated beau?]




SUITOR TUXEDO

Maybe I don't speak English as well as I thought, after all. This puzzle definitely made me second guess it. 
Glad everyone else found it so easy though!
I’m really troubled by the casual reference to violence against women in 9D as a stand in for seduction. Do better.
Okay I definitely got a chuckle out of FACTORFICTION.

This was, for me, like the start of a rollercoaster ride, where the car inches up the tall hill, straining and squealing, building anticipation, then comes that precious moment of zen at the top where the world stands still, followed by the bam-bam-bam rush of nonstop thrill.

So it went for me, filling in the box around the edges and getting some crosses in the theme answers but unable to crack them, working deliciously hard, until that wait-a-minute moment where the answer started coming, then it did, and immediately after, bam-bam-bam, all the other theme answers fell, and in the blink of an eye and a wow, the puzzle was done, and there I was sitting with a goofy smile, thinking, “What just happened?”

That’s a good puzzle.

Not to mention an appearance by the lovely-to-see three-S ROSSSEA, some sweet answers in SENSATE, SIDLED, TERTIARY, SLOE-EYED, and POISON PEN, and a clever, original, and tight theme.

An entertaining day-brightener you made, John.  Thank you and bravo!
"Be somebody. Be a ho." https://youtu.be/HKTmLd5PTyc?t=18&si=CapnO30_4UzKho1Y

Continuing the theme of "What day is it today?" A very fine Wednesday puzzle, but in no way a Thursday offering. Oh well.

Liked the theme and the thematic entries. FACTORFICTION was my favorite, but I'm a mathematician. Would have preferred the clue to have been "42 = 5 x 8". So much neater.

Never heard of OVERARM throwing, but maybe it's a thing. Some very lovely long vertical entries in the grid: MENAGERIE, TERTIARY, LOTHARIO, SLOEEYED, POISONPEN, TEXASTEA. Really great there.

Solved without help in about fifteen minutes, but missed  the trickiness that make Thursday puzzles especially enjoyable.

Came across this recently--an all-time favorite, rendered on [Baroque instruments]: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cPL-T_6NuPU&list=RDcPL-T_6NuPU&start_radio=1 "Cacao to 'cacao' ": https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AUqs6OGkUdE

Lovely puzzle, a tad too easy, but not enough to complain about.
Very amusing theme, and one that benefited from not having a revealer, (something I complain about sometimes).  The themers were all excellent.
And some excellent fill as well, with little that felt stale.  I especially liked the clue "Blue stop sign?".  Definitely worth a chuckle.
And I gave myself a (virtual) pat on the pack for getting TERTIARY with just one cross.
And some advice:  If you're going to put your report card in the trash, put it way down deep in the trash.  If it's just sitting on top,...

My complaint? 
It's not a Thursday theme.  You know it.  I know it. We all know it.
It's not a bad theme; it's a perfectly good theme.
It's just not a Thursday theme.

It was a great puzzle, but it was a Monday or Tuesday level at best. On Thursdays, I (and I’m sure many long-time/serious solvers) are looking for a challenge. Please give that to us. I finished this one in 7:29, way below my Thursday average.
Great wake-up puzzle to start the day, took me 10:05

I liked the theme clues and answers - clever and fun and useful in the solve.
Just one thing that nagged me: OH NO REASON. Phrases like this (i.e. not actual phrases, just a string of words sometimes seen together) seem almost cheating to me, but I understand it in American grids with so much open space needing "inventive" fill. But, the clues for these need to be really tight, otherwise it's just frustrating since there's no way to guess the answer without lots of crossers. At that point you may as well not have a clue at all. The clue for this one was not tight: it genuinely didn't help me get the answer, I just noticed what the string of words was in the end.
Apart from that, a great puzzle. It was satisfying to finally win the battle with the NE corner, despite the best efforts of bygone baseball players and teams and San Francisco streets and, oh, no other reason... Thanks John!

Even with four sports clues, I had no chance at a goldstar. NINA, OMAR, GOTO, OHIOAN was brutal.  Oh well. Newbie blues. Which brings up as to when 'blue' became a color for risque. I thought it was the red room of pain. Cheers.
This was not a Thursday puzzle.

27D

Puerto Rico is not a nation but a territory of the US.
5D and 21A put me in a pirate mood

Does hiding half of your answers behind question mark-riddled clues and specific trivia references that only you understand make you a better constructor? Does it create satisfying puzzles? Pardon the poison pen, but this puzzle was godawful.

I will say I enjoyed the puzzle, but not as a Thursday. I spent more time looking for the trick before accepting it was just wordplay. A good Thursday to send to someone just getting into crossword, if nothing else.

This is weak, but here goes…
Seamstress’ bathroom?
TAILOR HEAD

Now, THAT is a puzzle!   Very original.
Once again I find that starting from the bottom paid off nicely--not that I didn't traipse down the garden path a time or two...  For example, I STOMPed instead of TROMPing.  

Best of all, I remembered when TEXAS TEA was an unknown for me (maybe a couple of years back, actually.)  Also, it's handy to know words like LOTHARIO, which don't get used all that often.  

Trickiest clue prize goes to 30A Kind of a drag?  

I've never heard of Jimmy CARR; is he a comic we'd like to find of Netflix, or one of those shock-'em types....?  
For "I'm so mean I make medicine sick," I wanted Don Rickles... but I guess his day is over.
The wordplay in the theme answers is a delight!  I rushed through the puzz last night but now this morning on second look it is wonderful.  Gotta stop rushing!

A cute wordplay theme, nicely clued.  Though I can't remember exactly what puzzles he's created, I have this feeling that John Kugelman comes up with one fresh and imaginative idea after the other.  FACTOR FICTION alone, with its wry clue, is worth the price of admission.

I've always called the pitch OVERHAND, even though I always call the other one SIDEARM.  Very inconsistent of me.  But when I couldn't fit OVERHAND into the spot meant for OVERARM, I instantly suspected a rebus.  (I hadn't figured out the actual theme at that point.)

Re 16A:  What on earth was Ken Jennings talking about????????

I also don't get SAFE WORD.  Is it that the word itself isn't "blue", but the activity that it's "stopping" is?  A pretty convoluted clue, John.

But mostly I liked the wordplay in this puzzle and had fun trying to figure out the answers out in advance.
Whoa – all turned around this week. Puzzle was good fun, Wednesday it is but Wednesday it isn't! Thanks!

Good long workout for me, but a really cute theme. It was fun working out each of the theme answers. Two thumbs up for this one. And of course - a puzzle find. A Thursday from August 13, 2015 by Jim Hilger. There were four blank squares in the puzzle and those were the theme. Some examples: SHORTL_UNCH (LUNCH break) JA_IL (JAIL break) Some other theme answers: SPR_INGVACATION COMMER_CIAL HEA_RT STA_TION Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=8/13/2015&h=58a ....

7 Down…overarm? Really?
Sidearm? Yes.
Overhand? Yes.
Underhand? Yes?
Only began solving but I had give kudos for 16A 👏👏👏 Not only do I remember watching this as it happened but I, too, thought the same. Great minds think alike, or something like that, right?
ARSE is very common usage in the UK but not in Europe. And is not justified by saying the UK is part of or around Europe which it is surely not.

A dooking good theme. But rather gentle for a Thursday.


Entrance form?

DOORDIE


A singer refuses?

CANTORWONT

ARSE/PARSE, SENSATE/SENSEIS were nice echos for me of the theme wordplay. ARSE, HOE, SAFEWORD gave me a little buzz, which 42A squelched. 

Even I finished a little early for a Thursday, but CMON: if you think a puzzle is massively too easy  “for its day,” maybe wow us with your brilliance in your comment instead of just going AARGH?

Should I just tell you my comment, or sing it to you, loudly?

CONVEYORBELT?
Very easy Thursday

42 is always a correct answer

Country Home Critter = MANORMOUSE

Fun! I kept holding my breath, looking for a rebus, but it didn't appear, and when I solved MAYORMAYNOT, the cat was out of its proverbial bag, (or, the proverbial cat was out of its bag... or the prover... never mind.) and the rest fell into place. 

I don't hate the rebus puzzles, they are just more work, and I am lazy. Once I get their pattern, it's fine, it's the discovery that makes it "work."

Today's puzzle, with a fun theme, and real wordplay, made my morning! I loved the challenge, and the feeling of a satisfying Thursday. 

Now I have time to go out and enjoy this gorgeous day.
Well gosh I guess I have no complaints to contribute here. I thought it was a cute and fun Thursday, finished in a little over half my average. Enjoy the day.
30A is not clever and not witty, just objectively bad phrase-mining.

Today's poem made from words found in today’s puzzle
a/ walking of night by the whistling wire d/ radio comes in like a hole in the dark if you’re death say so with a little laugh a/ no reason not to
Awesome Wednesday puzzle!
I was desperately wanting to get MANOR MUPPET to work

Having been affiliated with many colleges and universities, I can state with confidence that the 

   "Start of many first-year course names" 

(48A) is virtually never the word INTRO.

But the start is frequently the *prefix* INTRO, so the clue is unassailable.

Cute, fresh, and way too easy for a Thursday.  Once again, I give props to the constructor while simultaneously bemoaning the swiftness of the solving experience.  As age and AI slop take their toll on my cognitive abilities, I have historically relied on the late-week Times puzzles (especially tricky Thursdays!) to provide a reliable weekly work-out as an antidote to incipient senescence.  Alas, the crunchy cruciverbal challenge seems to have become a thing of the past.

Wow I liked a lot about this puzzle even before I got into today’s Wordplay and really got the theme 100%! Love this theme <:) 

I had Casanova and then Loverboy before getting
LOTHARIO lol. 

As well as the themers I also liked OH NO REASON for “Why’s your report card in the trash?” and the last three letters of 13 D “Oil, they say.”: I thought it was TEXAS Gold but finally got the A in TEA.

Brilliant job, John Kugelman! I think that can’t have been easy to think of or execute. Great wordplay.
Did any other “Portlandia” fans try to fill in 32-Down with CACAO?
No idea why my comment from this morning was blocked. Perhaps because I said "chili dogs" instead of "coneys." Can an EMU be OHIOAN?

"I'm so mean I make medicine sick." - It's ALI, but sounds like something that would have been attributed to Chuck Norris, like "When he does pushups, the earth moves"

I do usually hope for more than 14 minutes of entertainment on a Thursday, but still it was a fun solve.

So many complainers!

I thought this was a fun one, great wordplay.
Still confused on Get ANA, and RID for Clear. Bum around Europe and Fly catcher had me going for a while.
Loved this funny puzzle!  More please.

One more puzzle find. A Sunday from September 27, 2009 by Patrick Berry with the title: "That is Two say." Some theme answers: TOOTHDK PHOTOSA CULATER EDGORME KCJONES RESTEZ QPDOLL POISONIV GREENWITHNV and AROUNDTHEWORLDINATDAYS Here's the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=9/27/2009&h=70a ....

My SW corner got off to a bad start bc I was sure the "national" drink of Puerto Rico had to be Bacardi rum, which fit. BZZZT. Eventually the crosses set me straight.

I enjoyed the puzzle very much, with several LOL answers for me. I'm surprised to see some negative comments here about John Kugelman's puzzles; he's one of my favorite constructors. To each his or her own.
I'm a little surprised this puzzle made the cut. Having a question-mark clue (32D) that is not part of the theme would be a deal-breaker if I were an editor.
Sorry, I haven't read all the comments, but am I the only one that had TENORTENDER for the Walking-around money for Pavarotti clue? I like it a bit better than the actual answer.
Great fun in this, wit too.
Very clever theme and quite challenging to boot!

Most enjoyable. 

[Lady of the house]
MANOR WOMAN

Delightful theme. Rhythmically, my Favorite was mayor may not. I couldn't finish it this morning because of middle upper section. I was thinking overarm was overhead with need for rebus . I had damn instead of main. Didn't know lothario without the crossings. Then aaagh came to me and all fell into place nicely. AARGH! That’s what elevated this from a Wednesday to a Thursday for me.

I really enjoyed this puzzle, despite a few stumbling blocks (I had dams for change the locks (lock and dam? Felt like a stretch because it was!)

Lots of smiles from the theme entries, and a bit of trivia I happened to know!
Great crossword! A pet peeve of mine is spelling TILL as 'TIL, but I understand it's common.
I really loved this puzzle! So many of the clues made chuckle. Really fun solve!

Quick, fun. 

I'm getting accustomed to quick Thursdays, given I don't have a lot of time to solve them. This one was a short splash of fun in the AM, interrupted by several hours of work on the job, then completed in another 15-second splash just now. 

My favorite was PASTORPRESENT given that I am a PK.
I have to throw a penalty flag on OVERARM.  I've heard of overhand, underhand, and sidearm, but never "overarm".

When I was reading tons of books as a kid, I probably never interpreted sloe-eyed correctly.   I think the adjective automatically made the character seem dumber as my brain mashed up 'slow' and 'doe-eyed'.  

Sloe gin comes from sloe berries and they just look like blueberries to me; if someone had those kind of eyes they wouldn't look very sultry
Salty because the residents of Rivendell are Elves not an Elf.

Comments on 'TIL' led me to one more puzzle find. A Monday from October 6, 1980 by Peter Swift. Three theme answers in that one - all with exactly the same clue: "Till____(a long time)" Those answers: HELLFREEZESOVER DEATHDOUSPART THECOWSCOMEHOME Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=10/6/1980&h=36a ...

And one more odd discovery. In pre-Shortz puzzles...

TIL

was clued as 'Sesame' 108 times. Furrowed brow - wasn't familiar with that usage. 

...
SLOE EYED was new to me! An enjoyable puzzle overall.

Thank you John Kugelman for a truly delightful puzzle. I can't say I saw the theme straight away, but when I did it brought me a big smile. Please keep them coming. I am sure my aging brain is benefitting from all the puzzles I do. 
Now I'm off to practice with my handbell choir, which is another thing that's good for my brain.
Be well my friends.
I enjoyed this one a lot!
An enjoyable puzzle, even though my rebus radar was disappointed to not be used again this week.

Okay - one more puzzle find - probably the oddest I've ever come across. A Sunday from December 19, 1974 by Frances Hansen with the title: "Living it Up." Five 23 letter theme answers in order: ADEFIANTOLDMAIDPRAYFORG IVEHERREMARKEDWITHABITO FAQUIVERTONIGHTISHALLSM OKEANDDRINKTILICHOKEAND NUTSTOMYLUNGSANDMYLIVER Whew. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=12/29/1974&h=44a ....

this is the kind of PUN theme we live for us lifelong solvers. I visit Rex does the xword site quite to join the chat often it's more of a haters crossword boxing ring whereas this chat site has a variety of takes on puzzles far more positive overall that's what I find fascinating where the 'grudge' factor comes into play when reviewing a puzzle or one's mood at the time of making a review. thanks John K. wonderful wry funny pun of a puzzle

Never heard the term overhand before. Google tells me it's a US thing. The rest of us use OVERARM.

I find it hard to believe that SLOEEYED is not an insult. I had a mistake at the end and I spent ages trying to figure out what was wrong with it, I assumed there was no way it could be a compliment. (Turned out it was a typo somewhere else.)

Difficult puzzle for me today. Some new words that I would never know: LOTHARIO?

However, it is a Thursday so I expect difficult. The theme was cute.

Rating 7/10

I really liked the theme! Very cleaver!

Unfortunately for me, there were several vital clues that eluded me. I think OVERARM was a big problem for me, as I couldn't get CLAY ART or similar out my head. As in, the style in which you throw the medium is your clay art.

But with a little Googling (not for answers but for knowledge, as is my cheating style) and finally a couple gimmes from the article, the beast was slain.

The very worst thing that could happen to a shipwrecked sailor if an iPhone arrived in the beak of a seagull that conveniently dropped it on the beach right in front of him and he picked it up hoping against hope that it did not, but it did?

LANDLOCKED

All the little chicks with the crimson lips
Go Cleveland rocks, Cleveland rocks!

I'm actually headed to Dayton this weekend for a wedding. Looking forward to destroying some Skyline chili dogs.

Well I don’t agree it was easy because it had a lot of sports and culture stuff I didn’t know but I got it after some work and loved the theme. What in the world is Lothario? I saw that LO and thought it had to be loverboy. And kind of a drag I had “race” for way too long. I like Astros way better than Colt 45s. 🙂

This puzzle’s sports hints were just not accurate. It seemed much more purposely misleading than most puzzles. 

IMO it was one of the weaker ones I’ve seen. 

But ty for always putting these together. I’d just try to be more accurate with direct hints like “overarm” which isn’t a thing. That’s “overhand” - we all like the ambiguous clues but when they are just wrong that’s meh.

Just chaĺlenging enough.

One person had an issue with overarching.  Ive never heard the term used, but, sure enough, the definition is overhand.  Fair enough.

I've also never heard of, or perhaps forget hearing of, a viol.  And I'm totally unfamiliar with lothario.  So that small section was for me the toughest.  The rest were easy to plod through.
Overarm? Never heard that used in all my years of watching sports.

Elon and his 20-yo tech bros illegally canceled science research and humanities grants by doing key word searches for references to women, ethnic groups, and any other term they deemed to be too “woke.” Regardless of the fact that the efforts of this wrecking crew were completely counterproductive to their stated goal of finding “savings,” it was an odious stunt that harmed and continues to harm real people. I refuse to answer 40A as clued so a DNF for me today.
So much good here! And loved the theme entries too. Perfect Thursday

Fun theme, good challenge, natics and unguessable names.
In hindsight I did know the name of a couple of Indian prime ministers.

Somehow solved this without lookups. Felt like a slog at times. Seemed almost like a themed Fri or Sat. Some of the many puns made me groan. Theme didn’t feel like a big payoff. Overall, I liked it though!
How have people not heard the word LOTHARIO???
I very confidently filled in taOIST for “fan of I statements” 😹😹😹
Had to double check a couple times that I was, in fact, solving the Thursday puzzle.

A real challenge for me today, and one I enjoyed. I got stuck at about 40% with no theme answers in place, and had to get a hint (actually, one theme answer) from Wordplay before continuing. Many clues were very enjoyable. Keep up the good work, John!

Lots of folks seem to be saying this was overly easy for a Thursday and let me just say … I disagree!!! I struggled quite a bit, perhaps because it took me a while to get the theme. But this was the first Thursday in a while where I felt I really had to work to break it in, and it didn’t start to flow until I’d finally figured out most of the theme answers.

@David Johnson

Over four decades for me.

@Francis

I pity the person who has to come behind and modify or debug code peppered with GOTOs.  Sadly, IBM's RPG language allowed GOTO statements, but thankfully most of the coders I worked with had learned to avoid them, but unfortunately not all of them.
@David Johnson "GOTO is considered harmful" since 1968. Thank Edsger Dijkstra for that guidance. Ik not even sure about switch statements to be honest ;)
@David Johnson Return Linefeed Rubout Rubout.

@Gary
Wow, nice. The algorithm behind OSPF!

@David Johnson

This clue/answer took me back to my days of writing BASIC code (later QuickBasic) and getting great results, albeit with plenty of spaghetti.  IF...THEN...ELSE GOTO (variable name, which was a step up from old BASIC line numbers).  Once, when programming with a friend/co-worker, we were stuck on a good variable name for the next subroutine so we just called it VARR (which is like naming your dog "Dog").

The memories are so pleasant that I thought for a moment I'd look and see if there were any free BASIC compilers out there I could tinker with for fun, but of course there is no task imaginable for which a new, nifty bit of code in an ancient language would be handy.

I am obsolete.  Not the first time that thought has arisen.

@Gary “not even sure about switch statements”

You, and me, and Martin Fowler.
@Don H Yes, BASIC code! My first college semester in 1978, I remember it well. I liked learning BASIC, we wrote a program to describe traffic patterns and cars queueing in a lane, if I remember
If I could get a down-and-dirty on the term "switch statements," I'd sure appreciate it.

@Francis
Ah, the folly of youth.

@Jerry
syntactic sugar for a bunch of if/else

@Katie You’re free to unsubscribe at literally any time.
@Miri Agreed, it’s a DRAG to have to hear these complaints daily.

@Miri one day the NYT comments managers at large, whoever they may be, will give us the ability to block other commenters, and all of our mutual dreams will come true. Until that day, if the two days of the week that are supposed to cater to experienced solvers fail to provide a challenge, those solvers will feel let down and are likely to express that disappointment in the comments.

The puzzle was fun. It didn’t belong in today’s slot. That’s not any reflection on the obvious skill of the setter; it’s the fault of whoever decided ito run the puzzle on a Thursday. In fact, I’d go so far as to say that is probably the most disappointing Thursday of the year so far. And I will in fact say it whether you want to hear it or not.

commentBody:

Of course I did. That same lyric is the reason I wasn't thrown by CAREER recently.

@Xword Junkie
But the clue represented FACTOR FICTION and FACT or FICTION quite nicely.  We all know that 42 is The Answer to everything, so that wouldn't have worked as FICTION.

@The X-Phile

Yes, we agree again. Scary, in a way. ;-)

@The X-Phile
It's not an old Thursday. It is the new and future Thursday, as long as subscriptions drive revenue.

@Alex

OH, NO REASON is an "actual" phrase, at least in my corner of the USA. It's a cute/evasive response to a probing "why" question---more or less like the question in the clue.

@Xword Junkie I'd definitely put it in the category of a string of words you sometimes see together in the situation you describe, but not an actual phrase. Especially with the "oh" at the beginning. Searching for it as a phrase gave me zero results apart from an AI generated answer that referenced a Quora question about something different.
@Alex 100% agree on OHNOREASON. Just a completely arbitrary phrase that has a tenuous connection to the clue. It creates artificial difficulty.

@Alex

Well, "my" A.I. says: The phrase "Oh, no reason" is a classic conversational deflection in American media, typically used when a character is hiding a suspicious, embarrassing, or chaotic motive. Because it is a ubiquitous idiom rather than a trademarked quote from a single project, it has appeared in countless films and television shows.

It gives several occurrences from The Simpsons, a NYT favorite. Here's one, from Season 2, Episode 14 ("Principal Charming"):

SCENE: SPRINGFIELD HIGH SCHOOL

(in the halls, we see "El Barto" is spray painted on a locker and on a bathroom door, Bart and Lisa are walking together)

BART: Ah, it's 3:00, so great to be done for the day, I feel so
      alive.
LISA: Don't forget, we have to pick up Maggie.
BART: D'oh! I hate even going near that old school.
(flashback to time frame similar to the present (1996 at the science fair, where Bart pulls out a test tube of mysterious liquid and pours it into Martin Prince's experiment, causing a huge explosion)
BART: You know, I still feel pretty bad about Martin...It was a
      terrible thing...well....in principle.
LISA: That's strange, why do you feel so awful about it?
BART: Oh, no reason.
LISA: I'll bet.

@Xword Junkie and @Alex
Yes! And delivered in a flutey, high-pitched voice as one was trying to be noncommital.....
@Alex it’s funny, I like the little surprises like this that come out of solving the other answers. Makes me chuckle. Like the blue stop sign. (Pineapple)

@Alex - Agree.  I certainly don't need each clue to be solveable by itself if the crosses can lend a hand (it is called a crossword after all), but I too am missing the connection between the clue and the answer.  I was expecting some sort of pun with a letter grade reference... something like "IDONTCIT" that would connect the idea of bad grades.
@Alex https://letmegooglethat.com/?q=%22oh%2C+no+reason%22

@D

"Artificial difficulty".   Quite a strange concept.

@WhySoMad? 
I suppose one could find at least a little humor in the idea that an absence of reasoning capacity might explain both the poor report card and the foolish notion that putting it in the trash would suffice to escape parental discovery.

@Joe
Welcome to crossword land, Joe, where characters from TV shows I never watched (eventually) surface when needed.  Did TEXAS TEA help? I bet you also knew the Colt .45’s changed their name. Was there a kerfluffle over that?

commentBody:
@Joe
NINA is a 20th C political reporter. OMAR was a character from a hit TV drama in the early 21st C. GOTO depends on a piece of 20th C science jargon. OHIOAN could derive from a late 20th C sitcom star (who prominently featured his home state), a movie star or a celebrity chef. Individually, each is trivia but they're scattered across time and various fields of endeavour.  The setter's goal should be to ensure that, even if you don't know A or B, you'll get C and D and can build from there.

@Jennifer

The Colt .45s became the Astros over 60 years ago. I doubt many people remember whether that was controversial. (Heck, the Astrodome, their previous stadium, was torn down years ago.)

@Eric Hougland

The Astrodome has been closed for decades, but it’s still there.

@Jennifer

The Colt .45s entered the National League in 1962, along with the New York Mets. After briefly changing their name to the Houston Colts (perhaps to distance themselves a bit from the guns and conjure more images of horses), they changed their name to the Astros when their permanent home, the Astrodome, opened. The celestial reference was due to the newly opened space center in Houston.

"A nation is a large, self-aware community of people who share common cultural, historical, or ethnic bonds, such as language, traditions, and ancestry. While often used interchangeably with "country," a nation focuses strictly on the people and their shared identity, rather than the physical borders or government."
Think Navajo Nation as an example. BTW, it doesn't hurt to look stuff up instead of being so decisively incorrect.
